subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective vs bipolar with psychosis discrimination
stem:
You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 26-year-old non-Aboriginal man brought in by his family after 8 weeks of bizarre behaviour. He describes persistent persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations of voices commenting on his actions. Over the last 3 weeks he has also developed elevated mood, decreased need for sleep, pressured speech and grandiose plans to start a new tech company.

His delusions persisted during a 5-week period when his mood and energy were normal earlier in this episode. He has had two previous similar episodes. There is no substance use confirmed on urine drug screen.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 92, RR 16, SpO2 99% room air, BP 124/76 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Pressured speech, distractible, grandiose, persistent auditory hallucinations of commentary type, persecutory delusions; no formal thought disorder, oriented to person place and time, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen negative, FBC UEC LFT TSH within reference ranges, head CT no acute findings, fasting glucose 4.8 mmol/L, lipids fasting cholesterol 4.6 mmol/L LDL 2.4 HDL 1.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder (bipolar type) given two or more weeks of psychotic symptoms occurring in the absence of a major mood episode together with current concurrent manic and psychotic symptoms over the course of this illness
options.rationale: DSM-5 schizoaffective disorder requires two or more weeks of psychotic symptoms in the absence of a major mood episode, with mood episodes present for the majority of total illness duration. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with psychotic features given the current manic episode with mood-congruent grandiose delusions and the absence of any persistent psychotic symptoms outside discrete mood episodes during the course of his illness
options.rationale: Psychotic symptoms persisted for 5 weeks during normal mood, which is incompatible with bipolar I psychotic features (where psychosis occurs only during mood episodes). (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophrenia given the 8 week duration of psychotic symptoms with persistent auditory hallucinations and delusions of persecution and the absence of negative symptoms that have lasted at least 6 months at this present time
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires significant mood symptoms to be brief relative to active and residual phases; sustained mania for 3 weeks excludes schizophrenia. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Substance-induced psychotic disorder given the relatively rapid onset of psychotic symptoms with concurrent mood elevation and the typical pattern observed in stimulant and cannabis use in young men of his age and demographic
options.rationale: Urine drug screen is negative and the persistence of psychotic symptoms during normal mood does not fit substance-induced psychosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder given the rapid onset of psychotic symptoms with full return to premorbid functioning expected within one month and no requirement for ongoing antipsychotic maintenance therapy beyond resolution of symptoms
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder is by definition less than 1 month duration; this presentation has lasted 8 weeks. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder is distinguished from bipolar I with psychotic features by the presence of 2 or more weeks of psychotic symptoms in the absence of a major mood episode. Mood episodes must be present for the majority of the total illness duration. Schizophrenia is distinguished by mood symptoms being brief relative to active and residual phases. Treatment of schizoaffective bipolar type uses a combination of antipsychotic and mood stabiliser. The diagnosis influences both pharmacotherapy and prognosis.
key_points: Schizoaffective: 2+ weeks of psychosis without mood episode plus mood episodes for majority of illness
Bipolar with psychotic features: psychosis only during mood episodes
Schizophrenia: mood symptoms brief relative to active and residual
Brief psychotic disorder: less than 1 month duration
Schizoaffective bipolar type: antipsychotic plus mood stabiliser
explanation.pearls: Lithium and lamotrigine are both reasonable mood-stabilising adjuncts in schizoaffective bipolar type; lithium has greater anti-manic and anti-suicide effect while lamotrigine targets depressive episodes with lower upfront monitoring burden.

stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 79-year-old non-Aboriginal woman with a 6-week history of anhedonia, early morning waking, 4 kg weight loss and pervasive low mood since the death of her husband. Her past medical history includes well-controlled type 2 diabetes, atrial fibrillation on apixaban and stage 3 chronic kidney disease (eGFR 42 mL/min/1.73 m2). She lives alone, has good social supports through her church and has no current suicidal ideation. Her GDS-15 score is 11/15. There is no cognitive impairment on MoCA.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6 °C, HR 72 irregularly irregular, RR 14, SpO2 98% room air, BP 132/78 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Tearful, psychomotor slowing, intact insight, no thought disorder, no psychotic features, GCS 15, MoCA 28/30
data_table.Investigations: Na 136 K 4.4 urea 6.8 creatinine 122 micromol/L eGFR 42, ALT 24 albumin 38 g/L, HbA1c 7.0%, TSH 2.4 mU/L, ECG sinus rhythm with QTc 460 ms, FBC within reference ranges

lead_in: Which antidepressant is the most appropriate first-line option?

options.text: Amitriptyline 25 mg nocte titrated to 75 mg over 4 weeks because tricyclic antidepressants have a long evidence base in older adults with biological depression features including early morning waking and significant weight loss
options.rationale: Tricyclics are avoided in older adults because of anticholinergic, cardiac and falls risk; they appear on the Beers list and STOPP/START criteria as potentially inappropriate. (Source: AGS Beers Criteria)

options.text: Sertraline 25 mg daily, titrating to 50 mg after 1 week with monitoring of serum sodium at 2 and 4 weeks, sleep and appetite, and review for response at 4 to 6 weeks before considering further dose adjustment
options.rationale: Sertraline is a first-line SSRI in older adults: no significant interaction with apixaban for bleeding (lower compared with paroxetine and fluoxetine), low anticholinergic burden, dose adjustment not required for moderate CKD. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Mirtazapine 30 mg nocte targeting appetite stimulation, weight gain and sleep restoration in geriatric depression with prominent vegetative features and minimal pharmacokinetic interaction with currently prescribed apixaban therapy at this stage
options.rationale: Mirtazapine is a reasonable second-line in older adults but is not preferred over sertraline as first-line; first-line remains SSRI per Australian guidance. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Citalopram 20 mg daily titrated to 40 mg over 2 weeks for fastest response with regular ECG monitoring to track QTc changes during dose increases over the next 6 weeks of antidepressant initiation and titration
options.rationale: Citalopram is restricted to 20 mg in adults over 65 due to dose-dependent QTc prolongation; her baseline QTc is already 460 ms. (Source: TGA citalopram QT prolongation safety advisory)
source_refs: TGA citalopram QT prolongation safety advisory

options.text: Venlafaxine extended release 75 mg daily titrated to 150 mg over 4 weeks combining SNRI efficacy with daily blood pressure monitoring and electrolyte review at 1 and 4 weeks for any hyponatraemia or treatment-emergent hypertension
options.rationale: SNRIs are reserved for second-line in older adults due to blood pressure effects and a higher discontinuation profile; SSRI is preferred first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: First-line antidepressant in geriatric depression is an SSRI, with sertraline typically preferred due to its favourable interaction profile with apixaban relative to other SSRIs, low anticholinergic burden and stability in moderate renal impairment. Citalopram is dose-restricted in older adults due to QTc prolongation. Tricyclics are avoided. Mirtazapine, venlafaxine and duloxetine are second-line. Monitoring of sodium at 2 and 4 weeks detects SSRI-induced hyponatraemia.
key_points: Sertraline is preferred first-line SSRI in older adults
Citalopram limited to 20 mg in over-65s due to QTc
Avoid tricyclics in older adults (Beers list)
Mirtazapine and SNRIs are second-line
Monitor sodium at 2 and 4 weeks for SSRI hyponatraemia
explanation.pearls: SSRIs increase upper gastrointestinal bleeding risk by approximately 2-fold; in older adults on concurrent anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy consider co-prescription of a proton pump inhibitor and document the risk-benefit discussion.

sources.label: TGA citalopram QT prolongation safety advisory

stem: You are the emergency department registrar reviewing a 68-year-old man brought in by ambulance with 12 hours of fever, dysuria, flank pain and rigors. He has a history of benign prostatic hyperplasia and an indwelling catheter inserted 3 weeks ago for retention. He has been unable to keep down oral fluids overnight. His Glasgow Coma Scale is 14. He looks unwell with cool peripheries and mottled knees.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.2 °C, HR 124, RR 28, SpO2 94% room air, BP 92/56 mmHg, capillary refill 4 seconds, urine output 10 mL last hour
data_table.Examination: Reduced peripheral perfusion, mottled lower legs, soft tender suprapubic region, right costovertebral angle tenderness, indwelling urinary catheter in situ with cloudy urine
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 3.6 mmol/L, FBC WCC 18.4 x10^9/L neutrophils 16.0, CRP 268 mg/L, creatinine 152 micromol/L (baseline 90), urinalysis pyuria nitrites positive, blood cultures and urine culture pending, chest radiograph clear

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Start oral trimethoprim 300 mg as a single stat dose plus 7-day course at home with general practitioner follow-up tomorrow morning, arrange community nursing for catheter change and oral fluid rehydration overnight
options.rationale: Septic shock with hypotension and lactate over 2 requires immediate IV antibiotics, IV crystalloid resuscitation and source control in hospital, not oral antibiotics at home. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis Management)

options.text: Send blood cultures only after the patient has received the first dose of intravenous antibiotics to maximise yield and avoid delaying antimicrobial therapy in the initial hour of sepsis bundle delivery in the department
options.rationale: Blood cultures should be obtained before antibiotics where this does not delay therapy beyond 45 minutes; pre-antibiotic cultures retain higher yield. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Wait for blood culture and urine culture results before commencing any antimicrobial therapy because empirical broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure may delay identification of the responsible organism and select for further resistance
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics must be given within 1 hour of recognition of septic shock; waiting for culture results increases mortality. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Send blood and urine cultures, administer 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid intravenously within 3 hours, give intravenous gentamicin plus amoxicillin or piperacillin-tazobactam empirically within 1 hour, monitor lactate clearance and start vasopressors if hypotension persists
options.rationale: The hour-1 sepsis bundle includes lactate measurement, blood cultures, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour, 30 mL/kg crystalloid within 3 hours and vasopressor initiation if MAP remains under 65 after fluid. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Start non-invasive ventilation with continuous positive airway pressure, give intravenous noradrenaline 0.1 micrograms/kg/min and intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g, and admit for source control work up tomorrow on the morning ward round
options.rationale: Vasopressors are added after a 30 mL/kg crystalloid trial in fluid-responsive sepsis; source control work-up should not be deferred to the next day in pyelonephritis with sepsis. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis Management)

explanation.summary: The hour-1 sepsis bundle requires lactate measurement, blood culture before antibiotics, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid within 3 hours for hypotension or lactate over 4, and vasopressor titration to a MAP target of 65 mmHg if hypotension persists. Source control is identified and addressed urgently. Australian empirical cover for urosepsis is typically amoxicillin plus gentamicin or piperacillin-tazobactam. Vasopressors are not commenced before adequate fluid resuscitation.
key_points: Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors as indicated
30 mL/kg crystalloid within 3 hours in hypotension or lactate over 4
Vasopressors target MAP 65 mmHg if hypotension persists
Source control identified and addressed within 6-12 hours
Urosepsis empirical: amoxicillin plus gentamicin or piperacillin-tazobactam
explanation.pearls: In catheter-associated urosepsis remove or change the catheter as part of source control; antibiotics alone without source control are associated with higher mortality and persistent bacteraemia.

subtopic_detail: Pulmonary embolism Wells score and age-adjusted D-dimer
stem: You are the emergency medicine registrar reviewing a 72-year-old non-Aboriginal woman with 24 hours of dyspnoea and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. She had a right total knee replacement 2 weeks ago and has been mostly bedbound at home. There is no haemoptysis, no calf swelling and no syncope. She has a past history of well-controlled hypertension on perindopril. She is afebrile and clinically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 102, RR 22, SpO2 94% room air, BP 138/82 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Mild tachypnoea, normal heart sounds, clear chest, calves symmetrical and non-tender, no signs of right heart strain
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia rate 102 no S1Q3T3 or right strain, chest radiograph clear, FBC and UEC within reference ranges, troponin not elevated, D-dimer 720 ng/mL (laboratory cut-off 500 ng/mL)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with oral apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily for 3 months because empirical treatment is more time-efficient than imaging given high pre-test probability and likely VTE in this clinical context
options.rationale: Wells PE score should be calculated first and confirmatory imaging obtained for moderate or high probability before commencing anticoagulation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Reassure that pulmonary embolism is unlikely because the chest radiograph is clear and the ECG shows no evidence of right heart strain or S1Q3T3 pattern and reassess at general practitioner clinic in 48 hours
options.rationale: Chest radiograph and ECG are insensitive for PE and should not be used to exclude the diagnosis. (Source: BMJ Best Practice Pulmonary Embolism)

options.text: Repeat the D-dimer in 24 hours and admit for observation overnight with serial troponin and ECG monitoring before deciding on definitive imaging or empirical therapeutic anticoagulation in the morning ward round
options.rationale: Repeating D-dimer adds no diagnostic value; the decision is to image or apply age-adjusted threshold (already exceeded here). (Source: BMJ Best Practice Pulmonary Embolism)

options.text: Calculate the Wells score (recent surgery within 4 weeks 1.5, tachycardia 1.5, PE most likely diagnosis 3 equals 6, high probability) and apply the age-adjusted D-dimer cut-off only if Wells is low or moderate before imaging
options.rationale: The Wells score arithmetic shown is incorrect; recent surgery scores 1.5 only if within 4 weeks AND immobilisation criteria are otherwise weighted; more importantly, high pre-test probability bypasses D-dimer and proceeds directly to imaging. (Source: BMJ Best Practice Pulmonary Embolism)

options.text: Calculate the Wells score, determine the patient is at moderate risk, apply age-adjusted D-dimer cut-off (age x 10 ng/mL = 720), find it exceeded and arrange computed tomography pulmonary angiography for diagnostic confirmation
options.rationale: Age-adjusted D-dimer cut-off for over-50s is age x 10 ng/mL; 72 x 10 = 720, which equals the D-dimer here so age-adjusted threshold is met and CTPA is indicated. (Source: BMJ Best Practice Pulmonary Embolism)

explanation.summary: Suspected PE workup uses the Wells score to estimate pre-test probability. Low-moderate probability patients have D-dimer testing using age-adjusted cut-offs in those over 50 (age x 10 ng/mL). High probability patients proceed directly to imaging without D-dimer. CTPA is the imaging modality of choice. VQ scan is reserved for renal impairment, pregnancy or contrast allergy. Therapeutic anticoagulation follows confirmation of diagnosis or pending imaging if high probability and no contraindication.
key_points: Wells score stratifies low, moderate, high probability
Age-adjusted D-dimer (over 50): age x 10 ng/mL
High pre-test probability: image directly without D-dimer
CTPA is first-line imaging; VQ scan if contraindication
Confirmed PE: anticoagulate (apixaban or rivaroxaban) 3-6 months
explanation.pearls: Age-adjusted D-dimer reduces unnecessary CTPA scans by approximately 12% in older adults with no decrease in sensitivity; the cut-off applies only in low-moderate Wells probability and only in patients aged over 50.

subtopic_detail: Acute kidney injury KDIGO staging and management
stem: You are the medical registrar admitting a 76-year-old non-Aboriginal man with a 5-day history of fever, productive cough and reduced oral intake. He has community-acquired pneumonia confirmed on chest radiograph and has been on ramipril 10 mg daily and rosuvastatin 20 mg daily for cardiovascular prevention. His baseline creatinine 4 months ago was 95 micromol/L. He has been unable to drink for the past 24 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 °C, HR 110, RR 24, SpO2 92% on 4 L nasal prongs, BP 102/64 mmHg, capillary refill 3 seconds, urine output 0.4 mL/kg/hour over 6 hours
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, jugular venous pressure not visible at 30 degrees, focal coarse crackles right base, no peripheral oedema, normal heart sounds
data_table.Investigations: Na 134 K 4.8 urea 14.2 mmol/L creatinine 198 micromol/L eGFR 28, FBC WCC 17 x10^9/L Hb 132 platelets 268, urinalysis no blood no protein, urine osmolality 620 mOsm/kg, fractional excretion of sodium 0.6%

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Continue ramipril for renoprotection given the patient's cardiovascular risk profile and proteinuria history, and add intravenous furosemide 40 mg to maintain urine output above 0.5 mL/kg/hour during the acute illness
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors should be withheld in acute kidney injury related to volume depletion; furosemide does not convert oliguric to non-oliguric AKI in this setting and may worsen hypovolaemia. (Source: KDIGO Acute Kidney Injury)

options.text: Commence intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg every 6 hours empirically for relative adrenal insufficiency in addition to broad-spectrum antibiotics and continue ramipril at the current dose while monitoring renal function trend
options.rationale: Empirical steroids are not indicated for AKI from pre-renal causes; ramipril should be withheld during acute volume depletion. (Source: KDIGO Acute Kidney Injury)

options.text: Arrange immediate haemodialysis given the rise in creatinine to over 2 times baseline meeting KDIGO stage 2 criteria with oliguria and pulmonary involvement to prevent further deterioration of renal function and clinical status
options.rationale: KDIGO indications for renal replacement (AEIOU: acidosis, electrolyte, ingestion, overload, uraemia) are not met here; stage 2 AKI alone without indications is treated with fluid resuscitation and removal of nephrotoxins. (Source: KDIGO Acute Kidney Injury)

options.text: Arrange an urgent renal tract ultrasound to exclude obstruction, withhold ramipril and rosuvastatin temporarily and commence intravenous N-acetylcysteine to attenuate any contrast or sepsis-related acute tubular necrosis insult
options.rationale: N-acetylcysteine is not recommended for AKI prevention or treatment in current guidelines; while ultrasound is appropriate, the key intervention is fluid resuscitation. (Source: KDIGO Acute Kidney Injury)

options.text: Diagnose KDIGO stage 2 acute kidney injury, withhold ramipril and rosuvastatin, give a 500 mL bolus of balanced crystalloid then 1 mL/kg/hour, treat the pneumonia, monitor urine output and reassess fluid status hourly
options.rationale: Pre-renal AKI from sepsis and reduced intake (creatinine 2-3 times baseline = KDIGO stage 2) is managed with withholding nephrotoxins, prompt isotonic crystalloid resuscitation, treating the precipitant and monitoring fluid responsiveness. (Source: KDIGO Acute Kidney Injury)

explanation.summary: KDIGO defines AKI by absolute creatinine rise of 26.5 micromol/L within 48 hours, 1.5 times baseline within 7 days, or urine output under 0.5 mL/kg/hour for 6 hours. Stage 1 is 1.5 to 1.9 times baseline, stage 2 is 2 to 2.9 times, stage 3 is 3 times or RRT requirement. Management starts with identifying the cause (pre-renal, intrinsic, post-renal), withholding nephrotoxins, fluid resuscitation if hypovolaemic, and treating the precipitant. RRT indications are AEIOU.
key_points: KDIGO stage 1: 1.5-1.9x baseline; stage 2: 2-2.9x; stage 3: 3x or RRT
Pre-renal AKI: withhold nephrotoxins, isotonic crystalloid, treat precipitant
Investigate cause: pre-renal, intrinsic, post-renal
Ultrasound if obstruction suspected
RRT indications (AEIOU): acidosis, electrolytes, ingestion, overload, uraemia
explanation.pearls: Fractional excretion of sodium under 1% supports pre-renal aetiology in oliguric AKI not on diuretics; if patient is on diuretics use fractional excretion of urea instead (under 35% suggests pre-renal pattern).
